slides.title: Why collections exist
body: A single variable holds one value. But real apps work with MANY values at once — every contact in your CRM, every track in a Spotify playlist, every message in a Slack channel.
Apex gives you three collection types: List, Set, and Map. Pick the right one and code stays simple. Pick the wrong one and you'll be writing extra logic forever.

vocabulary.def: A variable that holds many values, not just one — List, Set, or Map.

slides.title: List — ordered, allows duplicates
body: A List is an ordered sequence of values. First item is at index 0, second at index 1, and so on. You can have duplicates.
Real example: a Spotify playlist (order matters, same song can repeat). A Twitter/X feed (newest tweet first). The line at the DMV (first in, first served).

vocabulary.def: Ordered, indexed collection. Allows duplicate values. Zero-based index.
The position number of an item in a List, starting at 0.

slides.title: Building a List
slides.intro: Type this. Notice the angle brackets <String> — they tell Apex what type of values this List holds.
lineExplanations: Declare a List of Strings. List<String> is the type. 'new List<String>()' constructs an empty list. The new keyword always means 'create me a new instance.'
Append a String to the list. 'add' is a method on List that puts a value at the end.
Add another. The list is now ordered: index 0 is Bohemian Rhapsody, index 1 is Hotel California.
playlist[0] reads the first item. Square brackets are how you access by index. Prints 'Bohemian Rhapsody'.

slides.title: Set — unique, unordered
body: A Set is a bag of unique values. No duplicates allowed. No guaranteed order — you can't ask for 'the third item.'
Real example: your Twitter/X followers (you can only follow someone once). The set of unique email addresses for a marketing campaign. The set of distinct product IDs in a shopping cart.

vocabulary.def: Unordered collection of unique values. Adding a duplicate is silently ignored.

slides.title: Building a Set
slides.intro: Type this. Watch what happens when we try to add a duplicate.
lineExplanations: Declare a Set of Strings. Same shape as List, just the word changes.
Add an email. The set now has 1 entry.
Add the SAME email again. Apex silently ignores it — the Set still has 1 entry. This is exactly the dedup behavior you want for an email campaign list.
Prints 1, not 2. .size() returns the count.

slides.front: Your code collects every email that just bounced from a campaign. Some bounce twice. List or Set?
slides.detail: Set automatically deduplicates. With a List you'd have to write extra logic to filter duplicates yourself.

slides.title: Map — key to value
body: A Map stores key→value pairs. Look up a value by its key — like a phone book: name is the key, number is the value.
Real example: a URL shortener (short code → long URL). Salesforce trigger code constantly uses Map<Id, Account> to look up records by their Id without re-querying the database.

vocabulary.def: Collection of key-value pairs. Each key is unique; each maps to a value.
The unique identifier in a map entry — what you look up by.

slides.title: Building a Map
slides.intro: Type this. Two type parameters: Map<KeyType, ValueType>.
lineExplanations: Map<String, String>: keys are Strings, values are Strings. Construction follows the same new-keyword pattern.
put(key, value) inserts a pair. 'abc' now maps to 'https://salesforce.com'.
Add another mapping. The Map now has 2 entries.
get('abc') returns the value for that key. Prints 'https://salesforce.com'. This is the magic of Maps — instant lookup by key.

slides.title: Choosing between them
body: Order matters and duplicates allowed? List. Need to dedupe but order doesn't matter? Set. Need to look up by a key? Map.
Real example: a Salesforce trigger that processes 200 leads might use ALL THREE — a List of leads to update, a Set of unique account names already seen, and a Map of email→Lead for fast lookup.

slides.title: Iterating with for-each
body: The for-each loop walks every item in a collection. Shape: for (Type item : collection) { ... }.
Real example: every email-blast Apex job, every nightly batch process, every Salesforce trigger handler walks a collection like this.

slides.title: Looping a List
slides.intro: Type this. The for-each loop reads cleanly: 'for each name in names, do X.'
lineExplanations: Inline list literal — { 'a', 'b' } seeds the list with values right at construction. Cleaner than three separate .add() calls.
for (String name : names) — for each name in names. 'name' is a fresh variable that holds one item per iteration.
Runs once per name. Prints 'Hello Arthur', then 'Hello Ari', then 'Hello Tuan'. The order matches the List's order.

slides.title: Looping a Map
slides.intro: Type this. To loop a Map, iterate its keys and use .get() inside the loop.
lineExplanations: Inline Map literal: 'Alice' => 90 means key 'Alice' maps to value 90. The fat arrow => is just for map literals.
scores.keySet() returns a Set of all keys. Loop over the keys, then use .get() to fetch each value.
Prints 'Alice: 90' and 'Bob: 85'. Map iteration order isn't guaranteed — don't rely on it.

slides.title: Set and List helpers — addAll, contains, isEmpty
slides.intro: Type this. Three of the most-used collection methods. addAll merges one collection into another, contains checks membership, isEmpty guards a DML so you don't waste a statement on zero records.
lineExplanations: Set built from a literal of two strings.
List built from a literal of two strings — note 'sale' is duplicated across the two collections.
addAll merges every element of moreTags into tags. Duplicates are silently dropped (it's a Set). Now tags holds {sale, featured, new}.
contains returns true if the value is already in the set. O(1) lookup — much faster than scanning a List.
isEmpty returns true on a zero-length collection. Common DML guard: 'if (!toInsert.isEmpty()) insert toInsert;'

slides.front: Your trigger needs to update each Lead by its Id. You already fetched 50 Leads from the database. What's the fastest way to look one up by Id?
slides.back: Put them in a Map<Id, Lead>, then leadMap.get(someId).
slides.detail: Looking up by Id in a Map is essentially instant. Searching a List for a matching Id requires walking it — slow with 50,000 records.

slides.question: You're storing the unique tags applied to a blog post (each tag can appear at most once). Which collection?
slides.explanation: Set guarantees uniqueness automatically. List would let duplicates in. Map needs a value per key — overkill for a tag list.

slides.title: Why this matters in Salesforce
body: Apex governor limits cap how many database queries you can run per transaction (100 SOQL queries). The trick: query ONCE into a collection, then loop over the collection.
This pattern — query once, then loop — is how every well-built Salesforce trigger scales. We'll go deep on it in the Triggers lesson and again in the Governor Limits extras.

vocabulary.def: Salesforce-imposed cap on resource usage per transaction (queries, DML, CPU time, etc.).

slides.title: The bulk pattern in miniature
slides.intro: Type this. It's the heart of every well-bulkified Apex trigger.
lineExplanations: ONE query to the database. Returns up to 10 Accounts as a List. This counts as 1 SOQL query against the governor limit.
Walk the list with for-each. The variable 'a' holds one Account per iteration.
Read the Name field off each Account. No additional queries — we already have all the data.

slides.title: Capstone: State population map
slides.intro: Build a Map of state codes to populations, find the big states (>10M people), then sort them alphabetically and print each with its population. Demonstrates Map + List + Set together — exactly Ari's live demo from session 1.
lineExplanations: Construct an empty Map keyed by 2-letter state code, valued by integer population.
Add Massachusetts.
Add Florida.
Add Rhode Island.
Blank.
Empty list to collect 'big' states (population > 10M).
Loop the map's keys — keySet() returns an unordered Set of every key.
Pull the population for the current state. Map.get returns the value.
Filter — only states with > 10 million people.
Add to bigStates list.
Close the if.
Close the loop.
Blank.
Now build a sorted list. Sets aren't ordered — convert to a List first.
addAll copies every keySet element into sortedStates in one call.
List.sort() sorts in place. For Strings that's alphabetical.
Loop the sorted list and print each state with its population.
String concatenation forms 'MA: 6000000', etc.
Close the loop.

slides.title: What you now know
body: List for ordered/duplicates, Set for unique, Map for key-lookup. for-each loops walk all three.
Next: Classes — how to define your OWN types. Once you know classes, all of Apex (and Salesforce's entire SObject system) starts making sense.
